abundance: a measure of how many fish are in a population or a fishing ground
acoustic survey: systematic gathering of information on fish availability and abundance using underwater sound
anadromous: fish that live their adult lives in the ocean but migrate up fresh water rivers to spawn
aquaculture: the farming of freshwater and saltwater organisms including molluscs, crustaceans and aquatic plants
bathypelagic: the open ocean or pelagic zone from 1000 to 4000 meters below the ocean surface
benthic zone: the ecological region at the lowest level of a body of water including the sediment surface
benthos: aquatic organisms which live on or in the seabed
biodiversity: the variation of life forms within an area
biomass: the total weight of a fish species in a given area
bony fish: fish that have a bony skeleton and belong to the class osteichthyes
bottom trawling: a fishing method that involves towing trawl nets along the sea floor
bycatch: the harvest of marine life and seabirds during fishing operations when other fish were the target
carrying capacity: the supportable population of a species given food, habitat conditions and other resources
catadromous: fish that live their adult lives in fresh water but migrate down rivers to spawn in the sea
cohort: individuals of a stock born in the same spawning season
commercial fishery: umbrella term covering fisheries resources and the whole process of catching and marketing fish
demersal fish: fish that live in or near the bottom of a sea or lake
demersal zone: the zone at or near the bottom of a sea or lake
depletion: reducing the abundance of a fish stock through fishing
dredging: dredge designed to catch scallops, oysters or sea cucumbers towed along the bottom
ecologically sustainable development: using, conserving and enhancing fishery resources so that ecological processes are not degraded
ectothermic: animals that control body temperature through external means
endangered species: a population of an organism which is at risk of becoming extinct
fecundity: the reproductive capacity of a fish, usually measured by the number of eggs produced
fishery: the activity leading to the harvesting of fish, including the fish stock, the fishermen, and the business
fishing effort: a measure of how much fishing is being done, usually in terms of gear and time
fishing mortality: the rate at which fish are removed from a population by fishing
food chain: a sequence of organisms each of which uses the next lower member as a food source
forage fish: small fish which are preyed on by larger predators
growth rate: the increase in size or weight of a fish over time
habitat: the place where an organism lives
harvest: the total number or weight of fish caught and kept from a fishery
incidental catch: the catch of non-targeted species during fishing
landing: the amount of fish brought to shore and sold
maximum sustainable yield: the largest average catch that can be continuously taken from a stock
migration: the movement of fish from one area to another, often for feeding or breeding
mortality: the rate of death in a fish population from all causes
natural mortality: the rate of death from natural causes such as predation, disease, and old age
overfishing: fishing with a rate that exceeds the capacity of the stock to replenish itself
pelagic zone: the open water zone away from the bottom of the sea or lake
pelagic fish: fish that spend most of their life swimming in the water column away from the bottom
phytoplankton: tiny free-floating photosynthetic organisms in aquatic systems
population dynamics: the study of fish populations and how fishing mortality, growth, recruitment, and natural mortality affect them
precautionary principle: if an action might cause severe harm, the burden of proof falls on those advocating the action
predator-prey: a predator is a species that feeds on other species; prey is a species being eaten
primary productivity: measurement of plant production that starts the food chain
purse seine: fishing technique capable of harvesting large quantities of surface-schooling pelagic fish by surrounding the school
quota: the amount of catch that can be legally landed in a time period
recruitment: the number of new young fish that enter a population in a given year
selectivity: ability of fishing tackle to catch a certain size or kind of fish
shoaling: the behaviour of fish which aggregate together for defence, foraging, or mating
spawning: the production or depositing of large quantities of eggs in water
species: a group of organisms capable of interbreeding and producing fertile offspring
stock: group of fish of the same species that occupy a defined area of the ocean
sustainable fishing: fishing activities that do not cause undesirable changes in productivity, diversity, or ecosystem
sustainable yield: the catch that can be removed indefinitely without causing the stock to be depleted
trophic level: the position that a species occupies in a food chain
upwelling: the process by which cold nutrient-rich water rises from deeper to shallower depth
